channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - techraptor.net
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for techraptor.net

item.title: Memoirium Preview - King's Field Trip
item.content:encoded:
Before there was Dark Souls, there was Demon's Souls, but before even that, there was King's Field. FromSoftware's seminal (if creaky by modern standards) dungeon-crawling adventure served not only as an inspiration for the aforementioned games, but also seemingly for today's subject, Memoirium.

Taking place in a dreamlike landscape filled with surreal architecture and plenty of floating furniture, Memoirium melds the third-person exploration and combat of the Soulslike genre with the low-poly look and eerie atmosphere of King's Field (and, to a lesser extent, Shadow Tower).

I got to sit down with the demo ahead of its release as part of the latest Steam Next Fest extravaganza, and I came away tentatively excited to check out the full game, although there are a few issues that developer GoldenGratus will need to iron out prior to this one's debut in the big leagues.

Memoirium Convincingly Evokes King's Field

Memoirium bills itself as a Soulslike, but it's far better at evoking the woozy, dreamlike PlayStation-era vibes of King's Field than the ruined grandiosity of FromSoftware's later franchise.

I explored two key locations during my time with the demo: the tutorial area, which consists of a forest rendered in vivid reds and purples, and a more subdued academy with a panopticon-style light constantly scanning for undesirable elements (i.e. the player).

Both were atmospheric and compelling, managing to evoke the hazy, topsy-turvy logic of a dream without ever feeling arbitrary or convoluted.

That's also thanks in large part to the level design, which gives plenty of opportunities to wander off the beaten path to take on extra enemies and hunt down loot, although I'd have liked to see a few more surprises in the largely flat and linear school area.

Naturally, the demo doesn't give much of a sense of what Memoirium's story will look like; the Soulslike genre is well-known for its elliptical and ambiguous storytelling anyway, and this is a small slice of the finished product.

Nevertheless, I came away from Memoirium intrigued by its setting and excited to explore more of its winding corridors and nocturnal woods.

Admittedly, it can sometimes feel like it's lacking an identity of its own, particularly in the dialogue stakes; Memoirium falls back rather often on the fragmentary dialogue and lofty Proper Noun Usage that can sometimes get a touch tedious in the genre's lesser examples.

Still, the wobbly polygon aesthetic and environmental creativity on display made me want to delve deeper into Memoirium's world and uncover its secrets.

Combat Feels Wonky in Memoirium

It's a good thing, too, because Memoirium's combat is going to need some work before it's ready to come out of the oven.

Since this is a Soulslike, you know what to expect if you've played any previous games in the genre; expect lock-on-based fights that incorporate stamina management, watching for tells, and punishing mistakes.

Memoirium's combat is undone by one fatal mistake on this front. Its weapons simply don't feel like much fun to use.

In essentially every combat encounter across my time with Memoirium's demo, it was virtually impossible to land more than one or two hits on any enemy before they would inexplicably dance out of range or before the weapon I was using would simply stop hitting them.

This made regular enemy fights last much longer than they needed to and turned the two bosses I battled in the demo into frustrating, repetitive slogs, defined mainly by my patience running out rather than being pushed to the limits of my skill.

Landing blows in Memoirium doesn't feel particularly great, either. There's a weightless floatiness to the combat that will need to be addressed if GoldenGratus wants to compete in this insanely crowded genre.

I'm also a little worried about enemy variety, which has proven the killing blow for many other Soulslikes. I wish I'd been wrongfooted by more unusual, non-humanoid enemies in the Memoirium demo; with visuals this deliberately lo-fi, there's no real excuse for not getting creative in this (king's) field.

Memoirium Preview | Final Thoughts

I'm cautiously optimistic about Memoirium. It's got a unique aesthetic and plenty of charm and heart, and it's clearly been built with love for the Soulslike genre.

If it wants to stand out in a busy field, though, it's going to need to spruce up its combat and throw a few more enemy curveballs. Here's hoping the full version can do just that when it arrives in August.

Memoirium was previewed on PC using a code provided by the publisher over the course of around 90 minutes of gameplay. All screenshots were taken during the preview process.

Joe has been writing for TechRaptor for several years, and in those years has learned a lot about the gaming industry and its foibles. He’s originally an… More about Joseph
